# Appendix

## Code and data

Code snippets, data access links and supporting scripts used throughout the book.

## Introduction to Markdown

Markdown is a lightweight plain-text format that renders to formatted HTML (or PDF, Word, etc.). Most agent memory files, README files, and the chapters of this book are written in Markdown — so a basic reading knowledge is useful even if you never write it from scratch.

The essentials:

| Element | Markdown syntax |
|---|---|
| Heading | `# H1`, `## H2`, `### H3` |
| Bold | `**bold**` |
| Italic | `*italic*` |
| Inline code | `` `code` `` |
| Fenced code block | ```` ```r ```` … ```` ``` ```` |
| Bulleted list | `- item` |
| Link | `[text](url)` |

That covers most of what you'll encounter. For a complete reference, see the [CommonMark spec](https://commonmark.org/help/) or the concise [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org/basic-syntax/).

## Introduction to Quarto

Quarto is an open-source publishing system built on Pandoc. It extends Markdown with executable code chunks (R, Python, Julia, Observable JS) and compiles documents to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, or — as in this book — a multi-page website.

A minimal Quarto document looks like this:

````markdown
---
title: "My analysis"
format: html
---

Some prose.

```{r}
plot(1:10)
```
````

The YAML block at the top (`---` … `---`) sets document-level options. Code chunks are fenced with ```` ``` ```` and an engine tag (`{r}`, `{python}`, etc.). Everything else is standard Markdown.

To render from the terminal: `quarto render myfile.qmd`. In RStudio or Positron, click the **Render** button.

Useful starting points:

- [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/) — the official guide, well-written and searchable.
- [R for Data Science, Ch. 28–29](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) — gentle introduction in the context of R workflows.
- [Quarto for Scientists](https://qmd4sci.njtierney.com/) — a short, focused tutorial aimed at researchers.
